sentences.text: The neighbors, they gave us compliments.
Hitler is going to come.
He's going to kill you.
Not all of them, but some of them.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the other ones were thinking.
Some of them, they were speaking up.
Hitler's going to come on you.
There were sometimes incidents.
People-- for example, on a Saturday, they was all going for a walk.
They were attacked by boys.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what happened, they had opened up stores against the Jews, Polish people.
There was signs-- don't buy by Jews.
Buy by your people.
When the war broke out, every Jew was shaking.
We knew what is coming.
And something, a [INAUDIBLE], is going to [? cover us up ?] with murder.
But you couldn't help it.
Everybody was [? hoping, ?] maybe this, maybe that.
And then, some of them, they start to go to Russia, because the Bug, the river Bug was six kilometers from my town.
And my family didn't move, because we was a very close family together.
We couldn't-- only the death sentence could separate us.
And then-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --the Russians came.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans left.
The Russians came, and this was another problem.
The Russians went back, and the Germans came back.
In 1939 was a change.
There was an agreement between them.
The Bug, it's going to be the border, [INAUDIBLE] or something like that.
And--
This was about three weeks.
The Russians came.
They was around, and then--
This is in October already.
And then they went back, and the Germans came.
Then started [INAUDIBLE] plenty tsuris, plenty trouble.
You was going to the Russians, you was getting-- you know, it was a lot of terrible, terrible things.
Yes, I could tell you.
A lot of them, when they came back, they got arrested.
They go to Bialystok, over the Bug.
Whatever was at that time, [? they're ?] [? driven ?] in.
And a lot of them, the Russians sent them back, also.
When they got them on the border, they sent them back to the Germans.
Because they were afraid for the Germans.
We know it was the Germans already, what they're doing, what they did to the German Jews.
When?
The where?
This was on the border from--
I was in my town.
It was September the 1st, on a Friday.
Friday, the 1st of November.
That time?
The radio, the Polish radio-- it was a mobilization, soldiers.
My brother was raised.
They took him to the army-- Yankev, Jacob.
He was a couple weeks-- a week before, he was called in.
And what can I tell you?
Right away, when the Germans arrived, is [NON-ENGLISH] For now, the Jews.
Everybody get a different [NON-ENGLISH]
Food was still-- everybody had something.
The Germans came in.
And then they went back, and the Russians came.
Later, a couple weeks, the Russians went back, and the Germans came in.
Then started the big trouble for the Jews.
They took away everything.
They made me to open up the store, and the Polish people came, and they took everything with them, together-- a partnership, a corporation.
And they start to give us problems.
And you had to pay rent, and you had to pay-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
To the Germans.
The Germans took over the local government.
Then we had to pay taxes and rent and this and that.
It was Gestapo.
It was Volksdeutsche.
The Polish people worked for them, for the Germans.
They [? watched ?] the law to keep for the Germans, everything.
It was organized from [? before. ?] The local-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --municipal, and this was existing, but they worked for the Germans.
At first ?] by a lot of them, but they were [? sitting ?] about 20 Gestapos, I would say so.
About 20, maybe.
Till the last minute, when they disappeared.
Was changing for ups and downs, came in new ones.
They changed the people there, the Germans.
Miserable.
It was no life.
We just existed.
It was no life.
'Till the end. 'Till the end in 1942, where we went out.
The ghetto--
They pushed us in all together in the ghetto.
This was in 1941.
It was ups and downs with the ghettos.
They closed off the ghetto, and they opened up the ghetto, and they kept us together.
And the worst part of it started when they went [? on the ?] Russians, in June 22, 1941.
In our town, in [?
Konstantynow. ?]
With a section.
In this, we got to stay there.
You cannot go in another section.
It was a fence around.
Yeah, [INAUDIBLE] from the wire.
Yes.
Already was about 1,500 less, because a lot of them died already.
A lot of them had been out to Russia.
Maybe about 1,200 people.
The living condition was terrible-- four, five people in one room, six people.
Three families in a one bedroom, and a living room and a kitchen.
Three families.
Approximately what?
This was in 1940 to '42.
Yes.
My parents and my whole family.
Of course, I didn't go there.
I didn't have any-- it was no store anymore.
Of [NON-ENGLISH].
It's hard to explain, with what we was living.
We used to organize at night, go out to get something, to buy something, to give away a suit and get potatoes.
To Polish people, to the neighbors.
You picked up the good ones.
The other ones, the bad ones, they left you right away, and they brought you to the Germans.
It's very-- when it comes to food, it's very hard to say, because we didn't have food.
It was tsuris.
It was very bad.
We couldn't get medical supplies.
People was dying right and left.
There was no doctor there.
It was at the Polish side.
They wouldn't come.
Ther was never a Jewish doctor in my town.
tsuris-- very bad.
Typhus, sickness-- terrible.
Sure.
A lot of them died.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The SS.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Oh, they was not far away.
Yeah, sure.
They was watching.
They kept an eye on this.
That's all.
Yes.
It was this SS.
It was [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't think so.
There was maybe more.
They was in and out.
Yes.
Everyone has to wear an armband.
It was a white strip about four inches wide, and a Magen David from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]
Some of them, they were running away from the ghetto.
I used to run away, in the time, at night, when they used to come to get the people to slave labor.
They'd send them out to Majdanek, to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --Sobibor and so forth.
I used to run away at night, jump out, and I went in the woods.
It was bad.
What do you mean about organization?
What happened?
They took-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just to destroy the Jewish people.
They took them to labor camp, some far away, and they never come back.
They was killed there by the Germans, by the Polish watchmen, and so forth.
Yes, but I escaped a lot of times.
I used to jump out, run out through the window.
The houses were smaller houses.
One family had, like, a-- and I used to jump out of it and end up through the window, and I went out from the city-- a mile, two miles, whatever.
After they left, then I went back.
They had a list.
They was asking to give them a list, how many people-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --lives in that town.
This was in the beginning.

They gave them a list, the rabbi with a [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] --from the [INAUDIBLE], from the Jewish organization, and they took the list and brought them there, 18 miles to Biala Podlaska.
There was the residents from the state.
All the Jews.
No.
They went and they came at night, and they went from house to house.
There was a man.
They took them out.
That's all.
The Germans, with Ukrainians, with Polish.
With their helpers.
Did this at night, and then in the daytime, in the morning, 6, 7 o'clock, 8 o'clock, they took them to the-- they had to go 20 miles, walking.
Yes.
Some of them, sometimes, was a truck.
But sometimes they had to-- they went by foot.
[NON-ENGLISH]
That time, was my two brother-in-laws, they took them.
And they sent them away to Belzec.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They was there a few weeks, and they came back.
Somehow they sneaked out.
They didn't know where they're taking them.
But when they came to the place, where they stopped already to arrive, that they know where they are.
Some of them, we sent a Polish man after them to find out where-- where they took them.
Very few came back.
The other ones, they got killed there-- hard labor, no food.
The labor was unbelievable-- bad.
They're digging-- digging ditches.
They got killed there, that's all.
Nothing.
They took [INAUDIBLE], so they didn't take nothing with them.
My father was-- he took sick.
We couldn't take in a doctor.
We couldn't get medicine.
He passed away.
He's [INAUDIBLE] man, but he passed away, normal.
The normal time, he would be alive, if we had a doctor or medicine.
But that time was nothing, no doctor, no medicine, and he died six days before Purim, 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
He had pneumonia.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
My mother, when we was already out-- they sent us out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have to come later to it, because my mother-- we went from in our town-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --a day-- after Yom Kippur, 1942.
There was an order-- no, I'm mixing up.
The 6th of June, 1942, was an order from the capital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like every one Jew has to come into Biala Podlaska.
There is going to be a Judenstaat.
And we was thinking, this is the biggest lie in the world.
The Germans, they know how to frame up these things.
We start to run away.
We went to Sarnaki.
It was a different district.
To [INAUDIBLE], we went in the woods.
Just a very few.
Around the town, was all kind of names in the forest.
Was terrible to go away, to send the people to that.
We sent it with Polish people, with horse and wagon.
At night, we used to sneak out from the town.
I was doing a lot for my family, and another family and another family.
We start to run away from that town, because otherwise-- this was on a Sunday.
Till Friday, we had to be there in Biala Podlaska.
There is going to be the Judenstaat.
To [?
Lowicz, ?] Sarnaki, and in the woods, in the forest.
We organized.
We took farmers with the horse and wagon this way.
We tell them to go not in the main roads, only from the side streets, from the side roads.
And--
No.
We paid them in cash for the trip.
They picked up the best people, the best people-- we could trust them.
No.
The farmers, we picked up the best one, but we had [INAUDIBLE].
They didn't took away.
They took them there.
Yeah, they took something with them.
But what happened, that particular day, they didn't arrive to Biala.
They took 3,000 people, Jewish people.
They sent them to Sobibor.
How did they know?
They sent a Polish man to track where they're going.
And he came, and with the answer, and they went to Sobibor.
And there was already a crematorium and gas chambers, and they finished them up.
No, he direct didn't give me.
He gave it to the Biala.
It was 18 kilometers, but we find it out a day or two later.
If we would go there, we would be finished on June the 6th, 1942, after Shavuot.
Was a couple days.
We escaped.
We escaped.
And we tried--
My family was there.
It was spreaded, one in [?
Lowicz, ?] one in Sarnaki, one in the woods.
I was the only one near the town to know the news, what's going on.
In that particular [? week ?] was the older people.
They couldn't go no place, and they didn't know where to go.
They didn't have help.
They dressed up themselves in Yom Kippur, [NON-ENGLISH].
But Friday came a message.
The Germans were not going to send them out anymore to the Russians.
But this was not [INAUDIBLE].
They're supposed to go to Russia, but they went to Sobibor.
They're not going to do anything more.
They could come back.
And they started coming back.
We had so much tsuris to coming back-- terrible.
Everyone was attacking the Jews to come back.
Everyone-- a Polish, and the Russian prisoners.
Everyone was attacking.
Not beating.
They used to take away the [INAUDIBLE] from them, and a sofa.
It was very hard to come back.
OK, change it.
Tell me, how is my expression?
